title: Light a Candle for Them: Daily, Briefly
subtitle: A one-minute grief ritual for keeping presence without being swallowed by it
excerpt: A one-minute daily ritual for grief. Light a candle, say their name, say one sentence. Brief enough to be sustainable; enough to keep the connection present.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on candle-lighting traditions used across many cultures as a grief and remembrance practice.

practice.whenToUse: Use as a daily grief ritual in the weeks and months after a loss. Also useful on anniversaries, birthdays, and difficult days when the absence is particularly present.

content.text: A one-minute daily ritual for grief. It does three things: it marks that the person is remembered, it gives the grief a container, and it gives the day a specific moment of acknowledgement rather than a background current of loss running without a place to land.
Light a candle. It does not need to be special. A tea light on a kitchen counter is enough. The act of lighting, rather than the candle itself, is what matters.
Say their name out loud. Once. You do not need to say anything else yet. Just their name.
Say one sentence to them. It can be anything: what is happening today, something you miss, something you wish you had said. One sentence. Not a conversation, just a sentence.
Let the candle burn for as long as you have. Then extinguish it with intention: the grief is held, the day continues. Both can be true at the same time.
Original to homemade.education. Candle-lighting to mark grief and loss is found across many cultures.
